data.name: Diogo Costa
data.description: On 23 December, Porto fans received the Christmas present they had been asking for: the goalkeeper renewed his contract until 2030. "I wasn't sure if I would ever be a football player for Porto, but that has always been my biggest dream," he said at the time. He now has the highest salary at the club, but his release clause dropped from €75m to €60m. A major transfer is still on the horizon, but Diogo Costa has been a worthy successor to Vítor Baía, at club and national-team level. A European champion with Portugal at under-17 and under-19 level, as well as a Nations League winner, he now dreams of a World Cup title with the national team. He has been wearing the No 99 shirt at Porto but the Dragons' president, André Villas-Boas, recently asked whether he could play in the No 2 next season. That number carries an enormous importance at the club, traditionally worn by captains, and it would be a tribute to Jorge Costa, the former club legend and director who died in August after suffering a heart attack at the training centre.

data.name: Nélson Semedo
data.description: Left Wolves after five years in 2025 and had an approach to rejoin Benfica but felt it was too early to return home. Instead he chose to join Fenerbahce, with one of the main reasons behind that decision being that the Turkish club were coached by a certain José Mourinho. A month later Mourinho was sacked and joined … Benfica. The 32-year-old has Cape Verdean ancestry and was delighted when the country qualified for the 2026 World Cup. "Like Portugal, Cape Verde is a very small country, but with an abundance of quality and a will to win,” he said “and that was crucial for them to make history and qualify for the World Cup." Asked by Sky which black athlete had inspired him he said: “For me it is Muhammad Ali, he inspired me the most. He was a champion, he fought for us, he fought against racism during a very difficult time. He lost a lot of things himself but he gave us, black people, hope.”

data.name: Rúben Dias
data.description: Pulling his shorts up has become his trademark, especially when defending a corner. "It's a terrible habit. It's a concentration trigger, I do it unconsciously," he told the Portuguese FA TV channel. A natural leader, he is part of a generation that learned a lot from Cristiano Ronaldo about how to take care of their bodies. "The culture has changed and Cris helped a lot," Dias has said of the in the national team's environment. The defender, in fact, has even tried to inspire his girlfriend – the English TV presenter Maya Jama – with healthy routines, not only thinking about the physical aspect but also the mental one. Tremendously competitive, the 29-year-old says he is often doing a mental reset to wipe out past achievements. Having joined Manchester City from Benfica in 2020 he has won every conceivable title, including four Premier Leagues.

data.name: Tomás Araújo
data.description: Roberto Martínez wanted to avoid any controversy with this pick, saying: "It's not António Silva being left out, it's Tomás Araújo coming in." But the fact is, Araújo ended up taking his friend and club-mate’s place in the squad. The two players’ paths have crossed for some time now. A year older, Araújo made it into the Eagles' first team earlier, but then found it harder to establish himself. António Silva made an almost immediate impact later on, but then suffered a dip in form, meaning that, in recent months, Araújo was the first-choice playing next to Nicolás Otamendi. It was still a big call, though. Araújo had only three caps when the squad was announced, two of them coming in March and April. But the squad announcement went down very well at his family home, where relatives and neighbours had gathered to watch. His mum, Idalina, said: “When his name appeared, it was madness. An explosion of emotions among us and the friends who were here. I would love to go to the United States to see a game.”

data.name: Diogo Dalot
data.description: Dalot admits he’s been on an “emotional rollercoaster” since joining Manchester United in 2018. He fell out of favour in his second season, writing in the Players’ Tribune in June: “I was ashamed of sitting in the stands. We’d be watching the game in the directors’ box, and the fans right next to us would ask me: ‘Why are you not playing?’ I didn’t even know what to say. I wasn’t injured. Just not selected. I was so embarrassed that I started going down to the dressing room to watch the game on the TV alone. When I got home I barely even said hello [to his mother]. I’d go downstairs, fire up the PlayStation, put Paranoid by Post Malone on repeat, and start thinking and thinking … 'What am I doing here?'” He moved to Milan on loan in 2020, where he got to know Zlatan Ibrahimovic, who told him to invest in a padel business. “It was my first major investment on a personal level,” he told Forbes. Back at United a year later he worked hard to prove himself, and, in 2023-24, won players' player of the year. Comes from a musical family. His parents met in a choir, his sister Mariana is a professional musician, and Diogo is United’s dressing room DJ. Matheus Cunha: “Diogo is an unbelievable DJ … he plays a little part of the world for everyone.”

data.name: Matheus Nunes
data.description: Even as he joined Wolves from Sporting in 2022, he said Manchester City was his dream destination – and he had to wait only one year before he completed a move to the Etihad Stadium. Pep Guardiola gave the Portuguese a new lease of life, moving him from a midfield position to right-back, which has reopened the door to the national team. It took some time getting used to, though. "At the beginning I was stubborn,” Nunes told Dazn. “But after listening to different people and talking to my psychologist, I realised that adding different nuances to my game will only make me a better player." Guardiola believes Nunes can become one of the best right-backs in the world.

data.name: Cristiano Ronaldo
data.description: Cristiano Ronaldo may have chosen to play in Saudi Arabia instead of the US but in November he was still invited to meet Donald Trump at the White House as part of a visit by Saudi Arabia's crown prince, Mohammed bin Salman. Ronaldo's sixth participation at a World Cup (the same number as Lionel Messi) is unequivocal proof of his consistency, ambition and rage against time. So much so that no one dares to speak of an epilogue as his extraordinary career could still see him score his 1,000th goal. At 41, he manages to carry the feeling that there is still more to write, which is in line with the demanding nature he has always displayed. He is the leader, the point of reference, the bridge between generations. “Of course, I really want to win but honestly it won't change my standing in football,” he told Piers Morgan of the possibility of a World Cup triumph.

data.name: Bruno Fernandes
data.description: Fernandes revealed recently in an interview with the Portuguese FA TV channel that he refused to leave Sporting for rivals Benfica in the aftermath of the attack by Sporting fans on the club academy, back in 2018. Seven years later, he turned down an astronomical salary to join Al-Hilal in Saudi Arabia, even though he felt the Manchester United hierarchy was pushing him out: “The club wanted me to go. I told the directors that. I think they didn't have the courage to make that decision because the coach [Ruben Amorim] wanted me to stay. If I had said I wanted to leave, the club would have let me go.” Captain at United, he is also one of the leaders in the national team. He is also a staunch defender of Cristiano Ronaldo. “I know there are people who say the national team plays better without Cristiano, that the players are freer,” he says. “If that is the case, it is partly our fault. We don't need to be restricted just because he is on the pitch. He remains a top-level player inside the area and he draws defenders and creates spaces.”

data.name: Gonçalo Ramos
data.description: A striker who spends most of his time in the shadows. He almost always starts on the bench, for club and country, but is always ready to make a contribution when he comes on; his commitment often being converted into goals. In 2025, within the space of a week, he won the Champions League and the Nations League and, in between, became a father for the first time. Éder, the player who scored the goal that won Portugal Euro 2016, believes Gonçalo Ramos can do something similar at the World Cup. "Because of his mentality and the way he has managed his career at PSG I think he can be a super important player,” he said.

data.name: Bernardo Silva
data.description: To get some kind of variation of the usual praise Pep Guardiola heaps on a player he considers "irreplaceable" and with whom he wanted to renew for "many centuries", we can listen to what his national-team coach, Roberto Martínez, has to say. "For me, he's the perfect example of what a Portuguese player should be," he told the Stick to Football podcast. "He's a tactical person, he wants to have information, he wants to know where the space is. He knows how to play inside, he knows how to play outside. He's not a fast player, but he's very intelligent in how he draws the opponent out. Tactically, he's a gift; he can use both feet – and if you can do that then you're a competitor.” Joined Manchester City from Monaco in 2017 and has become a club legend, winning every possible trophy. Fans are now clamouring for him to have his own statue outside the Etihad Stadium. As Martínez says: "Usually you have the more tactical and creative player or a more competitive player. But then you can have both, and that's Bernardo Silva. If you want someone reliable, it's Bernardo. If you want someone marking the tallest player in the box, it's Bernardo." Signed a two-year deal with Real Madrid just before the World Cup.

data.name: João Félix
data.description: He may still be only 26 but it feels as if João Félix’s career has been standing still for some time now. After the 2024-25 season, which was split between Chelsea and Milan with equally modest returns, he signed for Al-Nassr. "I was about to return to Benfica for the last three summers, it was always my wish and I always told my agents that, but there was never a serious desire on the part of the club," he told Sport TV. The Benfica president, Rui Costa, did not agree, saying: "I'm very sorry that he said that. If it depended on Benfica, he would have returned three years ago. In those three years it was probably the case that Chelsea, Barcelona, Milan and now Al-NAssr wanted him more than Benfica.” Has delivered in Saudi Arabia with 33 goal involvements in 32 Pro League matches at the time of writing.

data.name: José Sá
data.description: The last year was anything but easy, with the death of several close people – including his “brother” Diogo Jota – but Sá has always faced adversity with grit, even professionally. He didn't succeed in Benfica's youth system, but ended up signing for Porto. He left the Dragão with little fanfare, but later managed to reach the national team and accumulate more than 150 games in the Premier League. In October, with Wolves struggling, he was filmed arguing with fans outside Molineux but won his audience over with his willingness to talk. The beard is his trademark and has been nicknamed António Variações after the renowned Portuguese singer who died in 1984 and sported similarly impressive facial hair. “They started to call me that after the European Under-21s [in 2015]. It must be the voice,” Sá once joked.

data.name: Renato Veiga
data.description: The son of Nélson Veiga, a former Cape Verde international who played in the Portuguese top flight, Renato joined Sporting in 2019 but did not make the first team. What he did, however, was to find an alternative route to the national team. He played for Augsburg and Basel before joining Chelsea. A loan spell at Juventus followed before he joined Villarreal in 2025. "I speak seven languages and I really enjoy learning them,” he says. “It helps me integrate more quickly into each country." One day, during one of his first training camps with the national team, he became very emotional when speaking about his late grandfather. "I am so grateful for everything he did for me," the 22-year-old said.

data.name: Gonçalo Inácio
data.description: The Almada president, Andreia Santos, perhaps tells the story best of how much Inácio means to the club where he started his career. "During one match, after Gonçalo had scored a goal, his mother, who was in the stands, went on to the pitch to celebrate. That goal, where he scored, now has his signature on it," Santos told A Bola.  At Sporting since 2012, he made his first-team debut in 2020 and is now a mainstay in central defence for his club. He has a discreet playing style but his effectiveness has earned him a starting place for his country too.

data.name: João Neves
data.description: It's the future that honours the past, through elegance and poise. The shirt remains tucked into the shorts, even after winning the Champions League at the age of 20. Neves benefited from the move from Benfica to Paris Saint-Germain in 2024 not only on the pitch but off it too, according to his former teammate Tiago Gouveia, who also left Lisbon for France (to Nice) a year later. "Signing for PSG did him a world of good. He was so addicted to sandwiches and supermarket lasagnas that it was starting to get worrying," he joked. The move may have changed Neves’s diet, but not his personality. "I go to the café and try to go unnoticed like an ordinary person,” he told Uefa. “I know that two or three people will recognise me and ask to take a picture, but I believe that the others will leave me alone. I don't have, for example, Vitinha's moustache." Says he always goes by his dad’s motto (he was a policeman): “The hardest thing is not getting there, it's staying there." Has reached such a level of appeal at such a young age that he was recruited -– together with his girlfriend, the actress Madalena Aragão – to inspire the Portuguese people to vote in the 2026 presidential elections.

data.name: Francisco Trincão
data.description: Trincão's impressive moustache has become his trademark. A very stylish dresser, he has even been on the cover of GQ magazine, but in the end he (mainly) wants to be known for the elegance he has on the pitch. The highlight so far for the national team? A double against Denmark in the quarter-finals of the 2025 Nations League, which Portugal went on to win. He is still looking to solidify his place in the national team and, apart from playing with Cristiano Ronaldo at international level, he shared a dressing room with Lionel Messi at Barcelona. "I have been able to play with the two best players in the world, each in their own style, and I learned a lot from what they transmit in the dressing room. It's very good to have experienced that." Back at Sporting now after his adventure in Spain, he also had one season on loan with Wolves in the Premier League.

data.name: Rafael Leão
data.description: "Zlatan created Leão," they say, as the Portuguese flourished under the Swede’s leadership and protection at Milan, especially the season they won the Scudetto together, in 2021-22. Sadly, though, the Swede’s retirement the following year did not lead to Leão becoming the team’s biggest star. Frustratingly inconsistent, he is capable of deciding games on his own and mesmerising fans with his skilful moves but can also disappear for long spells and twice angered the recently dismissed Milan coach, Max Allegri, with his attitude when substituted. Off the pitch he is continuing his career as the musician WAY 45. "Zlatan laughed when he heard it. He told me to focus on the football," Leão said once.

data.name: Pedro Neto
data.description: Pedro started out playing roller hockey in Viana do Castelo, on the coast near the Spanish border, but switched to football at age 10. He was the first footballer born in 2000 (or later) to play for the national team but took an unconventional route to the top, not playing for one of the big threes in Portugal, going from Braga to Lazio to Wolves before arriving at Chelsea in 2024. When the Portugal coach, Roberto Martínez, was asked on the Stick to Football podcast about comparisons between Neto and Eden Hazard, he said: "He's not like Hazard. Eden stopped the game to look for the one-v-one. Pedro is the opposite, he speeds up the game.” In March Neto hit the headlines for all the wrong reasons, pushing a ballboy during a game against Paris Saint-Germain. He was very contrite, though, and offered his shirt to the youngster after the game and escaped with a warning from Uefa.

data.name: Gonçalo Guedes
data.description: Scored the goal that secured Portugal’s first Nations League title in 2019 and was good at taking the limited chances he got with the national team but disappeared from the scene after joining Wolves from Valencia in 2022. Has not been able to find the consistency required to become a regular for his country, partly because of injury problems, and there was even a return to Benfica on loan in an attempt to rediscover his form. A move to Real Sociedad in 2025 reignited his career and his form in Spain earned a place on the plane to North America. "He's versatile, which is important for us,” Roberto Martínez said in March. “He's a different kind of forward, which is very interesting. I've already said that the striker positions belongs to Cristiano Ronaldo and Gonçalo Ramos so we're looking for a player with a different profile.”

data.name: João Cancelo
data.description: At the start of the year Cancelo made a surprise return to Barcelona and La Liga after a year and a half in Saudi Arabia with Al-Hilal. "For me, Barcelona is the best club in the world. It's the only club that gives me goosebumps," said the full-back, who has also played for Benfica, Valencia, Inter, Juventus, Manchester City and Bayern Munich. His CV leaves little doubt as to his quality but also hints at some instability – and that goes for the national team as well. "If we win the World Cup, I'll quit international football,” he said during the World Cup qualifiers. “Finishing strongly is always better than not winning any titles. If we don't win it this time, I'll try again.” After falling out with Pep Guardiola and leaving Manchester City, he told A Bola: “Lies were told. They were ungrateful to me. I never failed in my commitment to the club, to the fans and I always gave everything. I remember a time when I was robbed and attacked and the next day I was playing at the Emirates against Arsenal.” After securing the La Liga title in May he has won the league in four out of the top five leagues in Europe – only France is missing.

data.name: Rúben Neves
data.description: Now wears the No 21 for the national team as a tribute to his “brother” Diogo Jota, with whom he played at Porto, Wolves and the national team. Neves, like so many others in the national team, was devastated by the death of the forward last summer and on his left calf he tattooed an image of an embrace with his friend. At the funeral he carried the coffin and he wrote on social media: "More than a friendship, we are family, and we won't stop being that, just because you've decided to sign a contract a little further away from us. Whenever I go to the national team you will still be by my side at the dinner table, on the bus, on the plane. You will always be there with me, as usual." Neves broke through at Porto as a 17-year-old and tends to stay at his clubs longer than most players. After six years at Wolves he joined Al-Hilal in Saudi Arabia in 2023.

data.name: Rui Silva
data.description: "I loved scoring goals. Even at school I never went in goal," Silva told A Bola a few years ago when discussing his childhood. In the end he became a goalkeeper because of his height and has built his career through hard work. He trained with Sporting when he was 17 but was not offered a professional contract. It was only a decade later – and after spells at Nacional, Granada and Real Betis – that he joined Sporting and became a Portuguese champion. He was included in the Euro 2020 squad but did not play. He may travel to North America in Diogo Costa’s shadow but he now has a different status in the squad.

data.name: Vitinha
data.description: Last year was "unbelievable", according to the midfielder, who won the Champions League, Ligue 1, the French Cup, the French Super Cup and the European Super Cup with Paris Saint-Germain, as well as the Nations League with Portugal. To cap it off he finished third in the Ballon d'Or voting. "There were many moments in my professional career when I doubted myself a lot,” told A Bola, the publication who named him personality of the year. “It wasn't expected that Vitinha would become a great player or that he could achieve anything. At least, that's the feeling I always had. That's what helped me, it was my fuel.” In fact, he added, “Luis Enrique believed more in me than I did myself”. The PSG coach is not afraid to give the midfielder some “constructive” criticism, though. "My left foot is almost useless,” Vitinha says. “It's really bad. It's something Luis Enrique is on at me day and night to improve." A very humble person, he has said he wants to do everything he can to have the same influence with the national team that he has at PSG.

data.name: Samú Costa
data.description: He may have been part of Roberto Martínez’s squad for the spring friendlies but the 25-year-old was still the main surprise when the World Cup squad was announced. "He showed his energy, grit and tactical awareness in March," the coach explained. Samú was selected ahead of players with real international pedigree such as João Palhinha and Mateus Fernandes, and reached the national team without playing for one of the “big three” in Portugal. He came through the youth ranks at Braga but played only one game for the first team before moving to Spain. In February, he told A Bola that "it would be easier to reach the national team" if he were at a bigger club, adding: "The manager himself told me that.” But he managed it anyway. When the squad was announced he told Canal 11: "Perhaps no one expected me to be called up, and that was extra motivation for me. Being able to go against all odds and surprise people.”

data.name: Nuno Mendes
data.description: "I don't know if the perfect player exists, I don't think so,” the Paris Saint-Germain coach, Luis Enrique, said recently, before adding: “[But] if we think about a player with certain qualities in physical, technical and personality terms then Nuno clearly would be close to perfection." Despite the constant praise being flung at Mendes, by Luis Enrique and others, the defender continues to impress with his humility and discretion. "I don't like to say I'm the best. You have to show it on the pitch and I always try to do that," says the player who finished 10th in the Ballon d’Or ranking in 2025. Not bad for a left-back. Not even in his mid-20s, Mendes has already won the league in Portugal and France as well as the Champions League, the Intercontinental Cup and the Nations League. Has even made a habit of keeping Lamine Yamal in his pocket when facing the Spanish youngster.

data.name: Francisco Conceição
data.description: The tricky and fast winger left Porto after the falling-out between his father, Sergio, who was the coach, and the club president, André Villas-Boas, in 2024. He joined Juventus, where he wears the No 7 shirt that once upon a time belonged to Cristiano Ronaldo. "I didn’t get to speak to him before I joined,” Conceição told Sport TV. “But afterwards he said it is a huge club that demands everything from the player, a club that is used to winning, where you have to win every single competition you enter.” Francisco says his father was the best coach he ever had – "There are few like him" – but Sergio told La Gazzetta dello Sport that he had to use stick as well as carrot to get the best out of his son. “I gave him his debut during lockdown but he was a bit chubby so I said: ‘If you are hungry, drink water.’ To go far you need to make sacrifices and have a certain mentality."
